items.source: Neil Sethi / As We Approach The Open
items.title: Iran-US interim deal: Hormuz clears mines over 30 days, 60-day ceasefire, nuclear talks begin
items.idea: US and Iran confirmed an interim agreement Sunday. Iran clears mines from the Strait over 30 days. No toll during the 60-day ceasefire extension; after that, Iran charges for safety, navigation, environmental and insurance services. Iran reaffirms no nuclear weapons; talks on uranium dilution begin under IAEA supervision. US grants Iran oil sale waiver for the 60-day period. Sanctions relief is phased, conditional on nuclear progress. Iran publicly called this a US capitulation. SPX +1.2%, WTI at lowest since early March, DXY at 1-week lows, but 10yr yield oddly edging HIGHER despite the crude dump.
items.angle: The deal opens the pipe but does not restore Iran's supply (production already down 28%). The oddly rising 10yr yield alongside falling crude is a signal worth watching — the market may be pricing something other than inflation relief. Wednesday Warsh press conference is the next risk.
items.tweetA: Hormuz deal signed but nobody's reading the fine print. Iran clears mines over 30 days. No toll for 60 days — after that, Iran charges indefinitely for safety and navigation services. Iran is calling this a US capitulation. SPX prices peace. The bond market is pricing something else. $SPY $TLT
items.tweetB: Markets are pricing a peace deal. What's actually priced in: open Strait, no toll for 60 days, nuclear talks start. What's NOT priced: what happens if nuclear talks fail after 60 days. Trump told NYT he could restart military attacks. June 19 Switzerland signing is the next real event.

items.title: SPY gapped up on Iran deal — 740 is the gap fill level to watch
items.idea: SPY gapped up Monday morning following the Iran peace agreement. Friday's close above the 21 EMA confirmed end of the prior downtrend. There is now a large unfilled gap below at ~$740 SPY. The trade is not to chase the gap higher but to sell puts into the $740 level. The deal still requires a formal signing and things can flare up again within weeks if negotiations on final terms break down.
items.angle: Gap fills are one of the most reliable market patterns. $740 SPY is the magnet. If Warsh surprises hawkish Wednesday or the June 19 signing hits a snag, that gap gets tested. Selling puts into $740 beats chasing calls above the gap.
items.tweetA: SPY gapped up Monday. There is now a large open gap below at ~$740. Gap fills are statistically reliable. The trade here is selling puts into $740, not chasing calls above the gap. If the June 19 deal signing stumbles or Warsh surprises hawkish, that gap gets visited. $SPY
items.tweetB: Friday: SPY closes above 21 EMA, confirms end of downtrend. Sunday: Iran deal drops. Monday: gap up. Classic sequence. What comes next is almost always a gap fill attempt. $740 is the level. Either it holds and the bull case accelerates, or it fills and you get a much better entry.

items.title: Warsh's first FOMC: inflation 4.2%, bond market pricing hikes, White House demanding cuts
items.idea: Wednesday is Kevin Warsh's first FOMC press conference as Fed Chair. He faces: inflation at 4.2%, a bond market that has repriced toward hikes, and a White House demanding rate cuts. Gold is up 3% and has retraced LESS of its wartime move than any other major asset — a structural signal. The market has priced the Iran deal as an inflation cure; Warsh cannot. The Monday market snapshot: Nasdaq +2.1%, Brent -5.2%, DXY sliding 5th day in 6 sessions.
items.angle: This is the hardest first day on the job in recent Fed history. Any hint of tightening from Warsh derails the relief rally. Any hint of dovishness at 4.2% inflation risks bond market revolt.
items.tweetA: Warsh's first FOMC press conference is Wednesday. Inflation: 4.2%. Bond market: pricing hikes. White House: demanding cuts. He walked into a cage fight before the meeting even started — Trump hosted a UFC Octagon on the South Lawn Sunday. The market has not fully priced the complexity of Wednesday.
items.tweetB: The Iran deal gave the market a reason to rally. It did not give Warsh a reason to cut. 4.2% inflation doesn't get solved by a 60-day ceasefire. If Warsh sounds hawkish Wednesday, the gap-up from Iran gets filled. If he sounds dovish at 4.2%, the bond market will react. Pick your poison.

items.title: MiCA transition ends July 1 — ~75% of EU crypto firms may lose operating license
items.idea: The MiCA transition period ends July 1, 2026 — 16 days from now. Approximately 75% of European crypto companies may lose their operating license. This affects exchanges, issuers, and market makers operating across the EU. Either a massive last-minute compliance wave or a significant contraction in the European crypto ecosystem is coming.
items.angle: 75% of EU crypto firms losing their license in 16 days is underpriced. Watch for liquidity fragmentation in European crypto venues and spreads widening on EU-traded crypto assets post-July 1.
items.tweetA: July 1 is 16 days away. MiCA transition ends. Approximately 75% of EU crypto companies may lose their operating license. This is not being discussed enough. Either there's a massive compliance rush happening behind closed doors, or European crypto liquidity is about to get a lot thinner. $BTC $ETH
items.tweetB: 75% of EU crypto firms losing their MiCA license by July 1 is a regulatory cliff that makes the SEC enforcement era look gradual. European crypto exchanges, issuers, stablecoin operators — most are out of compliance. The question is enforcement speed, not regulatory intent.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Goldman: AI trade moves from theme beta to stock selection — crowding is now the key risk
items.idea: Goldman Sachs warns the easy buy-all-AI phase is over. The market is bifurcating: AI infrastructure names remain better supported, while application-layer and narrative-heavy AI names are being judged on ROIC, monetization timelines, and free cash flow visibility — not just capex scale or story. Hyperscalers are entering the prove the returns phase. Positioning remains very long (not panicked), meaning crowding is the primary risk if leadership wobbles or capital rotates.
items.angle: This is the same transition we saw in cloud computing circa 2021: theme beta worked until it didn't, then it was about which company actually had the earnings model. The AI infrastructure trade holds longer. The application layer needs to show unit economics.
items.tweetA: Goldman's call this morning: the easy buy-all-AI phase is over. Infrastructure stays supported. Application and narrative-heavy AI names now get judged on ROIC and FCF, not just capex scale. This is the same transition cloud had in 2021. Know which bucket your AI names are in. $NVDA $MSFT $GOOGL
items.tweetB: The AI crowding trade is still intact but the playbook changed. It is no longer theme beta — it is stock selection. Positioning is very long but not panicked. Crowding is the main risk, not fundamentals. One bad Mag7 print or a rotation trigger and the positioning unwind starts fast.

items.title: Haymaker trims Goldman Sachs after 130% gain — first outright sell in years
items.idea: Haymaker recommended Goldman Sachs in May 2024 at $460, reinforced the call in August 2024, and is now calling a sell at ~$1,080 — a ~130% gain not counting dividends. This is described as their first outright sale on a prior pick in some time. The reasoning is that there is more downside than upside over the next year or so.
items.angle: When the original bull who recommended a name at $460 says take profits at $1,080, that is worth listening to. $GS has been a beneficiary of M&A activity, trading revenues, and the overall risk-on environment. Warsh being hawkish Wednesday complicates the financials trade.
items.tweetA: Haymaker just sold Goldman Sachs after a 130% gain. They called it at $460 in 2024. It's at $1,080. They're out. When the original bull retires the position and says more downside than upside over the next year, I pay attention. $GS
items.tweetB: GS at $1,080. The 2024 call from Haymaker was one of the cleaner financial sector setups I saw that year. 130% later they are trimming. The financials trade has been spectacular. The question is what drives it from here — M&A, trading, or rates. Warsh on Wednesday tells you a lot.

items.source: Peter Boockvar / Pull forward of orders now over?
items.title: NY Manufacturing: new orders collapse from 22.7 to 3.5 — pull-forward is over, prices still rising
items.idea: June NY manufacturing index fell to 5.7 from 19.6. New orders collapsed to 3.5 from 22.7 in May and 19.3 in April — a near-total evaporation in one month. Inventories dropped to zero from 9.7. But simultaneously: the 6-month price received expectations jumped 8 points to the highest since April 2022. Capital spending plans hit a 5-month low at 10.9. The pull-forward of orders pre-tariff is definitively over. What is emerging is a stagflationary pattern: demand falling fast, pricing power still elevated.
items.angle: New orders at 3.5 is not a soft patch — it's a trapdoor. The 6-month prices-received at a 4-year high means manufacturers expect to keep raising prices even as volume craters. Classic demand-destruction stagflation. Watch for this to hit 2Q earnings for industrials.
items.tweetA: NY manufacturing new orders: 22.7 in May. 3.5 in June. That is not a slowdown — that is a trapdoor. The pull-forward of tariff orders is definitively over. Meanwhile the 6-month price outlook just hit the highest since April 2022. Demand falling, prices still rising. Stagflation is back on the menu. $XLI
items.tweetB: Boockvar's NY manufacturing data this morning: new orders collapsed from 22.7 to 3.5 in one month. Inventories zeroed out. Capital spending at 5-month low. And manufacturers' 6-month price expectations just hit a 4-year high. This is the worst possible combination for 2Q industrial earnings.

items.title: May Industrial Production: flat manufacturing, factory output grew in only one-third of categories
items.idea: May IP: +0.1% (vs +0.3% est). Manufacturing: +0.049% — least of the year. Bloomberg Economics: factory output increased only in about one-third of categories. However, April was revised up to +0.87%, the strongest since February 2025. Year-over-year: IP +1.67%, manufacturing +1.43%, both the highest since November 2025. Capacity utilization: 76.2%, best since July. Two narratives: monthly momentum stalling, annual trend firming.
items.angle: The divergence between monthly (stalling) and YoY (firming) is the key read. If the pull-forward in orders from Boockvar's data is real, the monthly numbers get worse through summer. The YoY trend will roll over with a delay.
items.tweetA: May manufacturing output: flat. Factory output grew in only one-third of categories. But April was just revised to the strongest month since February 2025, and YoY manufacturing hit the highest since November 2025. Two different stories in one data print. The monthly trend stalls right as the annual trend peaks. $XLI
items.tweetB: Buried in the industrial production report: manufacturing is flat month-over-month in May but year-over-year is the strongest since last November. These two things can both be true at a cycle peak. The question is whether the monthly stall is the leading indicator or the YoY trend is the real signal.

items.title: UAE exits OPEC after 49 years — structural fragmentation accelerates as Iran collapses
items.idea: UAE left OPEC effective May 1, 2026 after 49 years as a member. Quota-participating OPEC+ crude production rose +240 kbpd m/m to 24,866 kbpd in May — but including the UAE departure, overall output fell ~2 MMbpd. Iran's production is simultaneously collapsing: down ~28% from February 2026 levels under the US blockade, with further declines expected through June. OPEC is losing its two most significant swing producers simultaneously.
items.angle: The UAE leaving untethers the most capable, most pro-market Gulf producer from the quota system. They can now ramp production freely. Combined with Iran's supply collapse, OPEC's coherence is structurally weakening. Long-term oil-bearish (UAE unconstrained) but short-term Iran collapse partially offsets.
items.tweetA: UAE just left OPEC after 49 years. The most capable, most pro-market Gulf producer is now untethered from the quota system. They can ramp freely. Meanwhile Iran is down 28% from February levels under the US blockade. OPEC lost its two most significant swing producers in the same month. $USO $XLE
items.tweetB: The OPEC+ data deck without the UAE is a fundamentally different document. 49 years of Abu Dhabi inside the tent, now outside it. UAE's exit is long-run bearish for oil prices — unconstrained production from the Gulf's most efficient operator. The Hormuz deal doesn't change that calculus.

items.title: WTI symmetrical triangle breaks lower — next target $75-76, then gap fill to $67
items.idea: Despite a Strait of Hormuz partial closure that dragged from March through June, WTI crude was making lower highs while stocks made higher highs throughout. The multi-month symmetrical triangle in WTI resolved decisively lower. Next support: $75-76. Below that: gap fill to $67. From April through early June, weakening momentum and deteriorating price action were flashing warning signs. Price was right all along. Those who followed the narrative were wrong.
items.angle: The Hormuz doomsday narrative was the most widely discussed macro risk of Q1-Q2 2026, and the oil market just delivered a resounding no. Watch $75-76 as the near-term support line. A breakdown takes oil into the $67 gap fill scenario.
items.tweetA: WTI crude spent 3 months making lower highs while everyone was screaming about $200 oil. The symmetrical triangle just broke down. Next support: $75-76. Gap fill target: $67. Technical Analysis 1. Doomsday Narrative 0. Price was right all along. $CL $USO $XLE
items.tweetB: Every week from March to June: another Hormuz closure headline, another $200 oil forecast. Meanwhile the chart was drawing a symmetrical triangle with fading momentum. It broke lower. The market was smarter than the narrative the entire time. The chart was showing you.

charts.desc: WTI Daily — symmetrical triangle with multi-month trendlines resolving lower. 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Cui Bono: Iran won the peace, the petrodollar lost — long-term consequences of the Hormuz deal
items.idea: Phil Dauber applies Cui Bono/Malo analysis to the Iran deal. Winners: Iran — survived US and Israel militarily, won the peace politically, cash-flush with unfrozen assets, emboldened and still holding thousands of missiles and drones, facing less Arab opposition than before. Losers: Israel (a strengthened, revanchist Iran is an existential risk). US: the petrodollar — the linchpin of US global trade dominance since 1973 — is at accelerated risk. If oil trades in Yuan, the implications for US debt demand, Treasury flows, and capital inflows are medium-to-long-term possibilities that should not be ignored. Dauber: 'We could be in the middle innings of the run of the US Dollar as a reserve currency.'
items.angle: The market is treating this as a risk-on unwind. Dauber's non-consensus read: China's demand recovery post-Hormuz-reopening could reignite the very oil inflation the deal is supposed to cure. And a petrodollar in structural decline changes the demand outlook for US Treasuries.
items.tweetA: Phil Dauber applies Cui Bono to the Iran deal. Iran: survived US and Israel, won the peace, still holds thousands of missiles and drones, now flush with cash. US: gave away billions pre-negotiation for a vague nuclear promise. If oil eventually trades in Yuan, we may look back at June 2026 as when the petrodollar endgame began.
items.tweetB: The non-consensus read on the Iran deal: China's demand recovery post-Hormuz reopening could reignite the very inflation the deal was supposed to cure. The market priced supply relief. It hasn't priced the demand shock from the world's largest importer coming back to full capacity simultaneously.

items.title: Gold up 3% but retracing less of its wartime move than any other major asset — not a coincidence
items.idea: Nasdaq +2.1%. Brent -5.2%. Dollar sliding for the 5th time in 6 sessions. Gold up 3% and has retraced LESS of its wartime premium than any other major asset post-deal. The structural bid under gold — petrodollar reserve diversification, central bank accumulation, dollar reserve currency decline — is not going away because the Strait reopened. Gold is pricing something the equity rally is ignoring.
items.angle: If equities are pricing risk-off unwind, gold should be giving back its war premium fast. It isn't. Either gold is pricing deal uncertainty (could collapse after June 19), or it is pricing the petrodollar structural thesis — which is multi-year, not multi-week.
items.tweetA: Nasdaq +2.1%. Brent -5.2%. Gold +3%, retracing LESS of its wartime move than any other major asset. When risk-on hits and gold doesn't give back the premium — that is a signal. The structural bid is not going away because the Strait reopened. $GLD $GC
items.tweetB: The Iran deal should have crushed gold — risk-off trades unwind. It did not. Gold is up 3% and holding. Either the market doesn't trust the deal survives June 19, or it is pricing petrodollar reserve diversification as a structural multi-year shift. I think it's both. $GLD

items.source: Lord Fed / Between the Lines Vol. 11
items.title: SPX 8,000 summer target is a market-structure call, not a valuation call — Warsh is the key risk
items.idea: SPX opened the week at 7,440, dipped to 7,240, closed at 7,431. Everyone felt exhausted despite the market being essentially flat. Bull markets feel like a sequence of almost-tops. Lord Fed's thesis: 8,000 is a structural target, not a valuation target. The reasoning: SPX is less than 2% from ATH, SpaceX IPO was absorbed, broadening trade is starting under the surface, oil became a macro release valve, vol has backed off. At 8,000 you get panic-buying from underweight managers and hedge unwind. Mag7 is acting as a funding short — when it rallies, it adds oxygen. Key risk: Warsh tightening at his first press conference.
items.angle: 8,000 is what happens when the market structure forces buyers, not when a DCF says it's fair value. The most dangerous form of upside in a bull market. Mag7 as a funding short is the most interesting positioning observation of the week.
items.tweetA: SPX was basically flat last week and everyone felt like they had played 120 minutes a man down. That is the texture of a bull market. Lord Fed's 8,000 summer target: not a valuation call — it's what happens when underweight managers panic above ATH and the Mag7 funding short gets squeezed. Watch Warsh Wednesday.
items.tweetB: 8,000 on SPX is a market-structure target, not an earnings model. The logic: 2% to ATH triggers underweight panic. The next 3% is hedges monetized and shorts covering. The final stretch is levered ETFs, options, and systematic buyers all arriving at once. Mag7 is the fuel. Warsh is the only brake. $SPY $QQQ

items.title: Physical AI: from screen to physical world — Hyundai Metaplant, Waymo, FPV drones already operational
items.idea: NVIDIA's 2026 GTC reframed the next AI leg as AI that ACTS, not just predicts. WisdomTree's framing: ChatGPT in late 2022 made AI useful for generating information; 2026 makes AI useful for generating physical action. Already operational: Waymo in Phoenix/SF/LA fully autonomous; Amazon Zoox building purpose-built autonomous vehicles; Hyundai's Metaplant in Georgia runs hundreds of welding robots + automated guided vehicles, scales to hundreds of thousands of vehicles/year through machine coordination not labor. At the 2026 Winter Olympics, FPV drones tracked alpine and luge athletes in real time. New investable surface: humanoid robotics, drones, autonomous mobility, factory automation, enabling chips.
items.angle: The key insight is the transition point: once the intelligence chain runs all the way to physical output, the TAM stops being a software number and becomes an industrial number. Hyundai Metaplant is the clearest proof of concept — a factory DEFINED by technology, not augmented by it.
items.tweetA: Physical AI isn't coming — it's operational. Waymo in 3 cities. Hyundai's Metaplant in Georgia scaling to hundreds of thousands of vehicles per year through robot coordination, not hiring. FPV drones tracked alpine athletes at the Winter Olympics. NVIDIA's 2026 GTC made this official: AI now generates physical action, not just words. $NVDA $TSLA
items.tweetB: The ChatGPT moment for physical AI was this year's NVIDIA GTC. The difference between AI that predicts and AI that acts is the same as the difference between a GPS and a self-driving car. When the intelligence chain runs to physical output, the TAM becomes industrial, not just software. The next leg of the AI trade lives here.

items.title: US government takes equity in 9 quantum computing companies — $2B, first govt-as-VC
items.idea: May 21, 2026: US Commerce Department awarded $2 billion in grants to nine quantum-computing companies and took a minority equity stake in each in return. This is explicitly not a research grant, not a procurement contract — it is the federal government acting as a venture investor. WisdomTree's framing: a break from how Washington has historically supported emerging technology (DARPA contracts, NSF grants, SBIR awards all kept government at arm's length). Quantum is being treated as a strategic national security asset.
items.angle: The framing shift matters: when the government takes equity, quantum computing is no longer a science project — it's a strategic asset with alignment of financial interests. This creates a floor for sector access to capital and legitimizes it for institutional allocators.
items.tweetA: The US government just became a VC in quantum computing. $2B to 9 companies — and equity stakes in return. Not research grants. Not procurement. Equity. First time Washington has done this in any emerging tech sector. Quantum just went from science project to strategic national asset. $IONQ $RGTI
items.tweetB: DARPA contracts keep the government at arm's length. Equity stakes do not. The Commerce Dept taking minority positions in 9 quantum companies on May 21 is a policy regime change. Institutional allocators who needed a government backstop to justify quantum exposure just got one.

items.title: AI = top reason for 40% of US job cuts in May; top 1% of firms spend 650x more per employee than typical
items.idea: AI was cited as the primary reason for nearly 40% of US job cuts in May. The top 1% of US firms spend $7,450 per employee per month on AI versus the typical firm's $11.38 — a 650x spending gap. This is not a productivity story across all firms — it is an efficiency moat being built between companies in real time. The firms investing at $7,450/employee/month will structurally out-compete those at $11.38 within 18-24 months.
items.angle: The 650x spend gap is the most important number in this email. It means AI adoption is not an industry-wide uplift — it is a concentration of capability in a small number of firms building structural moats. The companies at $11.38/month are running their 2023 workflow.
items.tweetA: AI cited for 40% of US job cuts in May. But here's the real signal: the top 1% of firms spend $7,450 per employee per month on AI. The typical firm spends $11.38. That's a 650x gap. This is not an industry-wide productivity uplift. It's a structural moat being built in real time between the AI-haves and have-nots.
items.tweetB: Imagine two companies in the same industry. One spends $7,450 per employee per month on AI. One spends $11.38. They are currently competing in the same market. In 18 months they will not be the same company. The 650x AI spend gap is the most important competitive moat data point I've seen this year.

items.title: Cognition offers $10M performance guarantee: if AI agents don't deliver engineering value, they pay
items.idea: Cognition announced it will fund up to $10 million in credits if their AI agents fail to deliver the engineering value enterprise customers paid for. This is a performance guarantee on AI software — a first. It signals either extremely high confidence in current AI agent capability for software engineering tasks, or a customer acquisition strategy where the LTV of an enterprise customer justifies the insurance cost.
items.angle: Performance guarantees are the inflection point where a technology goes from promising to bankable. If Cognition can do this for software engineering, the next logical step is AI agent performance guarantees for legal, accounting, and scientific research tasks.
items.tweetA: Cognition just said: if our AI agents don't deliver the engineering value you paid for, we'll cover up to $10M in credits. That's a performance guarantee on AI software — a first. You only offer this when you're confident it works, or when the enterprise LTV makes the insurance trivial. Either way, it's a signal.
items.tweetB: The moment AI software providers start offering performance guarantees, the technology has crossed a threshold. Cognition is underwriting $10M of AI engineering output risk. The next step: performance guarantees for legal drafting, financial analysis, scientific research. SaaS just moved from access to outcome.

items.source: Ecoinometrics / Bitcoin's Rally Lacks Demand Support
items.title: Bitcoin bounces on Iran deal but demand data hasn't confirmed — headline vs genuine recovery
items.idea: The US-Iran interim agreement triggered Bitcoin to bounce alongside other risk assets Monday morning. Ecoinometrics poses the key question: is this the beginning of a durable recovery or just a headline reaction? Their analysis signals that rallies can look very convincing from the outside long before the underlying demand forces have actually changed. Over 34,000 institutional investors and fund managers are being told: wait for demand confirmation before concluding the worst is behind us.
items.angle: Every Bitcoin rally from March to June 2026 has had this same texture: fast, narrative-driven, unconvincing on demand. If on-chain demand signals don't confirm in the next 48-72 hours, this is the same pattern: headline bounce, then retest.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin is up on the Iran deal. But the Ecoinometrics question is the right one: is this demand coming back, or is the market just reacting to a headline? Every rally from March to June looked convincing on the surface before rolling over. Wait for demand confirmation, not price action, before calling this a bottom. $BTC
items.tweetB: Rallies that start with geopolitical news and end with demand disappointment are the most common pattern in crypto. Bitcoin is reacting to Iran deal headlines. The on-chain demand data hasn't confirmed. 34k institutional investors in this newsletter are being told to wait. I agree.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (demand flow data)

items.title: Musk: 'I don't think dollars will be used as currency. Just mass and energy' — alongside UFC bonuses in USD1
items.idea: Elon Musk stated: I don't think dollars will be used as currency. Just mass and energy. On the same day, the UFC reportedly paid some White House fight bonuses (from Trump's 80th birthday UFC event on the South Lawn) in Trump-backed USD1 stablecoins. These two data points in the same 24 hours: Musk sees the future as physical resource-denominated, and the US is piloting stablecoins as an alternative official currency. The petrodollar erosion thesis gets a philosophical confirmation from the richest man in the world.
items.angle: Musk's statement connects to SpaceX's energy-at-scale ambitions (100 terawatt space data centers) and his explicit modeling of the Banks Culture (where abundance is solved and dollars are irrelevant). The UFC-USD1 angle is more immediate: government-adjacent official payments in a private stablecoin is a policy signal.
items.tweetA: Elon Musk: I don't think dollars will be used as currency. Just mass and energy. Read that next to Phil Dauber's note on petrodollar erosion from the same morning. Then read it next to the UFC paying White House fight bonuses in Trump-backed USD1 stablecoins. Something structural is shifting, and it is happening fast.
items.tweetB: The future currency is mass and energy, per Musk. Meanwhile the UFC is paying bonuses in a Trump-backed stablecoin at the White House birthday fight. The dollar's competitors are not the yuan or the euro. They are tokenized claims on physical reality. That is a weird sentence that might be exactly right.

items.title: June 19 Switzerland: Iran deal formal signing — the date the bounce may run out of juice
items.idea: The Iran-US interim agreement is not yet signed — it is expected to be formally signed June 19 in Switzerland. Iran has stated the Strait will not reopen until the deal is signed. Markets have front-run this event. Blocmates flags June 19 explicitly as perhaps that's the date this bounce runs out of juice. This is the classic buy the rumor, sell the news setup. Also: oil executives are separately warning about oil shortages even if Hormuz stays open, suggesting underlying supply tightness beyond the geopolitical closure.
items.angle: June 19 is the date on the calendar that matters. If it goes smoothly, that's the sell-the-news day. If it hits a snag — Iran has given itself room by conditioning Strait reopening on signed ink — you get a violent reversal in every asset that priced peace.
items.tweetA: June 19 in Switzerland. That's when the Iran deal is formally supposed to be signed. Markets have already front-run the announcement. Iran says the Strait stays closed until the ink is dry. June 19 is the sell-the-news date or the reversal catalyst. Either way, mark it on the calendar.
items.tweetB: Oil executives are warning about supply shortages even if Hormuz stays open. The deal removes the geopolitical premium but doesn't fix the underlying production problem — Iran is down 28%, the UAE left OPEC, and pull-forward demand surge created inventory distortions. The Hormuz deal is not an oil supply fix.

items.title: SpaceX comp: $7.5T valuation + 1M Mars colonists, OR 100 terawatt space data centers — else $54,080/yr
items.idea: a16z's inaugural Profiles of the Future issue profiles SpaceX. Musk's compensation package has two vesting conditions: (1) SpaceX reaches $7.5 trillion valuation AND establishes a permanent Mars colony of at least 1 million people; or (2) SpaceX operates space data centers drawing at least 100 terawatts — more than 1,000x the consumption of every data center on Earth combined. Miss both targets, Musk earns nothing but his $54,080 annual salary since 2019. The board approved this because Starlink went from $0 to $11.4 billion in revenue in a few years, and every previous impossible Musk prediction has been right on direction if aggressive on timing.
items.angle: The 100TW space data center target is the one that connects to everything else happening in AI right now. Earth's energy constraints on compute are a real ceiling. Space-based solar power beaming down to space data centers removes that ceiling. This is the long-run answer to AI energy scarcity.
items.tweetA: Musk earns nothing at SpaceX except $54,080/year unless (a) $7.5T valuation + 1 million people on Mars, or (b) space data centers at 100 terawatts — 1,000x every data center on Earth. Starlink went $0 to $11.4B in a few years. The board approved this because he keeps making the impossible predictions come true. $SPCX
items.tweetB: 100 terawatts of space data centers. That's 1,000x total Earth data center power consumption. That is Musk's alternate SpaceX comp condition. Earth has an energy ceiling for AI compute. Space does not. The long-run answer to AI energy scarcity is not nuclear or solar on the ground — it's solar in orbit. $SPCX $NVDA

items.title: SpaceX's drone ships named after Banks Culture AI novels — Musk is building toward a specific utopia
items.idea: SpaceX's three autonomous drone ships are named after sentient AI starships in Iain M. Banks's Culture novels: Of Course I Still Love You, Just Read the Instructions, and A Shortfall of Gravitas. At the 2023 UK AI Safety Summit, Musk said: The Banks Culture books are by far the best envisioning of an AI future. Nothing is even close. The Culture is a post-scarcity civilization where superintelligent AIs manage orbital habitats and humans are free to pursue meaning. Four prerequisites: access to a star's energy fraction, physical intelligence at scale, cheap digital intelligence, cheap mass transport off-planet. a16z argues SpaceX is systematically building toward each of these prerequisites.
items.angle: Musk has been publishing his AI utopia vision on the side of his landing pads since 2016. The Culture's prerequisite list maps almost perfectly onto SpaceX's current roadmap: Starlink (digital intelligence distribution), Starship (mass transport off-planet), the 100TW space data center target (stellar energy), and SpaceX manufacturing (physical intelligence at scale).
items.tweetA: SpaceX's autonomous drone ships are literally named after AI starships from Iain Banks's Culture novels. Musk in 2023: The Culture books are the best envisioning of an AI future. Nothing is even close. He has been publishing his AI utopia target on the side of his landing pads since 2016. Most people just thought they were cool names.
items.tweetB: The Culture requires four things: stellar energy, physical intelligence at scale, cheap digital intelligence, and cheap off-planet mass transport. Starlink handles #3. Starship handles #4. The 100TW space data centers handle #1. SpaceX manufacturing handles #2. Musk is not building rockets. He is building the prerequisites for the Culture.

charts.desc: Photo: 'Of Course I Still Love You' catching Falcon 9 first stage — April 8, 2016, first successful drone ship landing in history. Named after a sentient AI starship in Iain Banks's Culture novels. 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Tariff refunds began in May — upcoming consumer sector earnings catalyst not yet modeled
items.idea: Barry Knapp at Ironside Macroeconomics flags that tariff refunds began flowing in May 2026 and are likely to boost earnings and margins for consumer sectors. The market has already moved on from the energy spike (the note references: Figure 7 — The market has moved on from the energy spike, so should the Fed). The tariff refund tailwind has not yet been fully modeled into Q2 earnings estimates for consumer-facing companies.
items.angle: Tariff refunds are a one-time margin boost that will show up in Q2 earnings for retailers, consumer discretionary, and goods importers. The market has not priced this because it has been focused on energy and geopolitics. This is a low-visibility earnings beat catalyst for 2Q reporting season. $XLY $XRT
items.tweetA: Tariff refunds started flowing in May. Barry Knapp at Ironside is flagging this as an upcoming earnings beat catalyst for consumer sectors. The market has moved on from the energy spike. The refund tailwind hasn't been modeled into Q2 estimates yet. $XLY $XRT — watch the 2Q earnings surprises.
items.tweetB: One of the most underappreciated 2Q catalysts: tariff refunds. They started in May. They'll show up in Q2 earnings as margin surprises in consumer discretionary, retail, and goods importers. Nobody is modeling these because everyone was focused on Hormuz. That is exactly the setup. $XLY

items.title: Knicks win first championship in 50+ years — community alchemy and the future of NFTs as proof-of-presence
items.idea: Jordi Visser attended Game 4 of the 2026 NBA Finals (Knicks vs Spurs) — the largest comeback in Finals history. His essay connects the experience to a framework about community: championships don't erase the pain of decades of loyal fandom — they redeem it. The pain is not erased. It is redeemed. The piece extends to NFTs as the technology for proving community membership — verifiable, permanent evidence of having been part of a community at a specific moment.
items.angle: Visser's observation about community alchemy has broad applications: NFTs as proof-of-presence/community tokens, sports franchises as cultural flywheel engines, and the psychological bond between fans and communities as the original network effect.
items.tweetA: The Knicks just ended a 50-year drought with the largest comeback in Finals history. Jordi Visser was in the building. His take: championships don't erase the pain of bad seasons — they redeem it. That alchemy is why sports communities are different from any other kind. And it's the best argument for why community NFTs have value.
items.tweetB: Visser's point on community after the Knicks win: The pain is not erased. It is redeemed. That sentence describes exactly what makes sports fandom — and communities built around shared suffering — so powerful. NFTs as permanent proof of presence at the moment of redemption. That's the use case that works.

items.title: Iran production down 28% from February — Hormuz deal reopens the channel, not the pump
items.idea: Iran's crude production collapsed ~28% in May from February 2026 levels under the US blockade, with further declines expected through June. The Hormuz deal reopens the transit channel but does not immediately restore Iran's production capacity. These are two different mechanics: transit lane vs. production ramp-up. Even with Hormuz open, Iran needs months to restore production levels. Supply bulls expecting a quick Iran supply surge are confusing reopening the channel with restarting the pump.
items.angle: The market is pricing Hormuz reopening as an immediate supply relief event. The production data says it isn't. Iran's supply ramp takes months, not days. The near-term oil supply picture is defined by UAE-unconstrained production increases, Iran's gradual ramp from depressed levels, and OPEC+ fragmentation.
items.tweetA: Iran's crude production is down 28% from February levels. The Hormuz deal reopens the channel. But the channel and the pump are different things. Even with the Strait open, Iran needs months to restore output. Supply bulls pricing immediate Iranian relief are confusing the pipe with the production capacity. $USO
items.tweetB: The Iran deal is a Hormuz story, not a supply story. Iran was already being blockaded at the production level — down 28% in 4 months. Reopening the Strait doesn't restore production overnight. Oil executives are separately warning about shortages even with Hormuz open. The supply picture is more complex than the headline relief.

items.source: McKinsey TMT / Brendan Gaffey & Michael Park
items.title: Europe's tech competitiveness gap: corporate-scale-up partnerships as the structural fix
items.idea: McKinsey's TMT practice argues for stronger, more systematic partnerships between European corporates and scale-ups as a path to enabling innovation to reach commercial scale and stay based in Europe. The subtext: European VC cannot fund the late-stage gap alone, and European scale-ups routinely cross the Atlantic for Series C and beyond. Corporate balance sheets as late-stage venture capital is a structural workaround. Adjacent McKinsey articles cover gaming and agentic software delivery.
items.angle: McKinsey naming this signals the European competitiveness debate (post-Draghi report) is shifting from regulatory to structural capital market reform. Corporate-scale-up partnerships are the workaround, but the real fix is EU capital markets integration. Partnerships are a band-aid on a structural problem.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's prescription for Europe's tech competitiveness gap: systematic corporate-scale-up partnerships. The subtext: European VC can't fund late-stage, so corporates need to step in. The real diagnosis: Europe has the innovation but the capital markets leak it to the US at scale stage. Partnerships are a band-aid.
items.tweetB: Europe keeps producing great companies and then losing them to US listings and US capital at the growth stage. McKinsey says the fix is corporate-scale-up partnerships. I think the fix is EU pension fund capital deployment reform and cross-border listing harmonization. Both can be true. Only one is structural.
